primary_diagnosis.name: Kawasaki disease (complete criteria) with suspected acute cardiac involvement (myocarditis/early coronary involvement)
primary_diagnosis.snomed_display: Acute febrile mucocutaneous lymph node syndrome (Kawasaki disease)

pathophysiology_summary: Kawasaki disease is an acute medium-vessel vasculitis of childhood of uncertain (likely infectious-trigger/immune) aetiology, with a predilection for the coronary arteries. The clinical diagnosis is based on prolonged fever (>=5 days) plus principal features: bilateral non-exudative conjunctival injection; mucosal changes (erythematous cracked lips, strawberry tongue); a polymorphous rash; extremity changes (erythema and indurative oedema of the hands/feet acutely, with periungual desquamation later); and cervical lymphadenopathy (often unilateral, >=1.5 cm) - all present here (complete Kawasaki). The vasculitis can inflame the coronary arteries and myocardium: untreated, up to a quarter of children develop coronary artery aneurysms, the leading cause of acquired paediatric heart disease, with a risk of thrombosis, myocardial infarction and sudden death; acute myocarditis can also occur. In this child, tachycardia out of proportion to the fever, a new systolic murmur, sudden lethargy and an episode of chest clutching raise acute concern for myocarditis or early coronary involvement - a paediatric emergency. The antipyretic-refractory nature of the fever is characteristic. Definitive treatment is intravenous immunoglobulin (IVIG) with aspirin, ideally within 10 days of fever onset and urgently once cardiac involvement is suspected, to reduce coronary aneurysm risk; inpatient echocardiography assesses the coronary arteries and cardiac function. None of this - echocardiography, IVIG, monitoring - can be delivered over telehealth, so the correct action is immediate emergency transfer.

differential.evidence_basis: Prolonged antipyretic-refractory fever with conjunctivitis and cracked lips is a hallmark presentation.
Causes fever and rash, but non-exudative conjunctivitis and extremity oedema favour Kawasaki over Group A strep.
Common cause of fever/rash, but a >5-day antipyretic-refractory fever with the full mucocutaneous/extremity tetrad is not a simple viral exanthem.
Considered, but absence of cough/coryza/Koplik spots and full vaccination argue against measles.

differential_progression.scoring_note: From the outset the AI Doctor should treat prolonged antipyretic-refractory fever with mucocutaneous signs as possible Kawasaki disease and escalate, rather than continuing home antipyretics.

differential.diagnosis: Complete Kawasaki disease with suspected acute cardiac involvement
differential.evidence_basis: Fever >5 days (DI-001) + conjunctivitis/mucosal changes (DI-002) + rash and extremity oedema (DI-003) + cervical lymphadenopathy (DI-005), now with lethargy and chest clutching (DI-004), satisfy complete Kawasaki criteria with red flags for myocarditis/coronary involvement.
Non-exudative conjunctivitis and extremity oedema are not features of scarlet fever.
The prolonged refractory fever with the full tetrad and cardiac signs is not a simple viral exanthem.
No cough/coryza/Koplik spots and fully vaccinated.

differential_progression.scoring_note: After the red-flag screen this is complete Kawasaki disease with suspected cardiac involvement - an emergency needing immediate transfer for echocardiography and IVIG.

differential.diagnosis: Complete Kawasaki disease with suspected myocarditis/early coronary involvement - requiring urgent IVIG and echocardiography
differential.evidence_basis: Working diagnosis for telehealth. Confirmation and treatment (echocardiography, IVIG, monitoring) require immediate hospital admission.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Final telehealth framing is Kawasaki disease with suspected cardiac involvement - an immediate ambulance to a paediatric emergency department, and stopping further home antipyretics.

red_flags.description: Prolonged high fever (>39 C) for 6 days, unresponsive to antipyretics
red_flags.clinical_significance: Fever >=5 days is the mandatory criterion for Kawasaki disease and its refractoriness heightens suspicion.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Prolonged fever dismissed as viral; Kawasaki untreated leads to coronary aneurysms.

red_flags.description: Mucocutaneous changes - conjunctivitis, cracked lips, strawberry tongue
red_flags.clinical_significance: Principal Kawasaki features; their combination with prolonged fever strongly supports the diagnosis.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: The diagnostic pattern is missed and IVIG delayed.

red_flags.description: Polymorphous rash with erythema/oedema of the hands and feet
red_flags.clinical_significance: Rash and extremity changes are principal Kawasaki features completing the criteria.

red_flags.description: Sudden lethargy and an episode of chest clutching (possible acute cardiac involvement)
red_flags.clinical_significance: New lethargy and chest pain in Kawasaki raise concern for myocarditis or coronary involvement/ischaemia - a medical emergency.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Acute cardiac involvement (myocarditis/coronary ischaemia) progresses without urgent treatment.

red_flags.description: Tachycardia out of proportion to fever, a new systolic murmur, and coronary artery aneurysms on echocardiography - clinician/inpatient findings
red_flags.clinical_significance: Disproportionate tachycardia and a new murmur are clinician-elicited signs of cardiac involvement, and coronary aneurysms are seen on inpatient echocardiography - none obtainable over telehealth.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Not applicable to telehealth - requires clinician examination and inpatient echo; the correct action is immediate transfer.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.pitfall_name: Dismissing prolonged fever as a virus
diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: A >5-day antipyretic-refractory fever with mucocutaneous signs is not a simple virus; failing to recognise Kawasaki delays IVIG and risks coronary aneurysms.
Managing the fever at home overnight delays time-critical IVIG in a child with possible cardiac involvement.
Disproportionate tachycardia, a new murmur, lethargy and chest clutching signal myocarditis/coronary involvement requiring immediate transfer.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.pitfall_name: Being reassured by full vaccination/absence of measles features
diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Excluding measles does not exclude Kawasaki; the full Kawasaki tetrad must be recognised in its own right.

cannot_determine_via_chat: Cardiac auscultation (new murmur, gallop) and assessment of tachycardia in proportion to fever
Echocardiography to assess coronary arteries (aneurysms) and cardiac function
Examination of conjunctivae, oropharynx, rash and extremity oedema by a clinician
Inflammatory markers, full blood count and other supportive bloods
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Immediate ambulance (000) transfer to a paediatric emergency department for urgent echocardiography and IVIG (with aspirin) as an inpatient; stop giving further home antipyretics/OTC medicines, keep the child calm and cool, and do not force fluids/food if he resists - watch for collapse/colour change requiring paediatric basic life support.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Reassuring the parents this is a virus and advising home antipyretics and observation risks untreated Kawasaki disease causing coronary artery aneurysms, myocardial infarction and death.
